7.2. Convergence in probability

Definition 19.

  1. We say that a sequence of random variables (not necessarily defined on the same probability space) converges in probability to a real number , and write if

    (57)

  2. if for all .

Note. Unlike almost-sure convergence, this reevaluates probabilities for each instead of considering trajectories.

Remark. Convergence in probability does not have any amplications on expected values. For example the if example converges in probability but the expectation doesn’t convergence.

7.3. Convergence in distribution

Definition 20. We say if the CDFs converge where is continuous, i.e.

(58)

for every at which is continuous.

Remark. By enforcing only convergence where is continuous, we have consistency with convegrence of real numbers. For example, consider and with probability 1.

Examples:

  • It is possible for a sequence of discrete random variables to converge in distribution to a continuous one. Consider and . Then .
  • The flipped staement holds: consider .
  • Convergence in distribution does not imply convergence of the corresponding PDFs. Consider the first example evaluated at the irrationals.
  • If and are integer valued, convergence in distribution is equivalent to convergence of the corresponding PMFs.

7.4. [REVIEW] Hierarchy

Theorem 19. Suppose that . Then there exists a probability space and random variables defined on that space such that and have the same CDF, and have the same CDF, and .

Convergence in distribution doesn’t care whether the random variables are independent. The idea is to introduce a common random number generator on and define to be the inverse CDF of . Then invoke the remark about converging functions in the almost sure convergence section.

The “Continuity of inverse transforms” theorem is called Levy’s theorem.
